### [Isomorphic Labs reveals proprietary model scientists call 'an AlphaFold 4'](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00365-7)

Isomorphic Labs, the DeepMind drug-discovery spin-off, revealed a proprietary AI model that Nature reported scientists are calling 'an AlphaFold 4'. The model represents a major advance in computational drug discovery, but – unlike the original AlphaFold releases – it is not open source. External researchers have been left without access to the model weights or a clear replication path.

The decision to keep the model proprietary marks a strategic inflection. AlphaFold's open release in 2021 generated enormous goodwill and academic adoption, but Isomorphic Labs is a commercial entity with drug-discovery partnerships to monetise. A proprietary model creates a data and capability moat: pharmaceutical companies that want access must partner with Isomorphic directly, and the model's training data – drawn from DeepMind's research infrastructure – cannot be replicated by competitors without equivalent investment.

For biopharma companies evaluating AI partnerships, the question is whether Isomorphic's proprietary advantage justifies the dependency. Companies that built internal pipelines on open AlphaFold tools now face a widening gap with a closed model they cannot inspect.

### [Jefferies survey: AI still makes up less than 10% of most R&D budgets](https://insights.citeline.com/scrip/digital-technologies/ai/ai-still-makes-up-less-than-10-of-most-rd-budgets-finds-jefferies-survey-TUTCJC4JPFGMXJAKZE3YTIIKII/)

A Jefferies survey of 50 biopharma companies found that only 30% currently allocate more than 10% of their R&D budgets to AI. The finding is striking given the volume of AI announcements, partnerships and pilot programmes the sector has produced over the past two years. AI is appearing as a dedicated line item – a change from 2024 when it was typically buried in broader technology or informatics budgets – but the absolute numbers remain small.

The data helps explain a structural tension. Vendors and platform companies are shipping agent-capable tools that promise to automate portions of target identification, clinical trial design and regulatory filing. But the buyers – pharma R&D organisations – have not reallocated capital at a pace that matches the supply of new tools. Budget cycles in pharma move slowly: most R&D allocations are set 12 to 18 months in advance, and AI spending competes with clinical-stage programmes that have defined milestones and regulatory timelines.

The survey suggests that for AI to take a materially larger share of R&D budgets, it will need to show returns within existing budget cycles – not promise returns in future ones. Agents may be ready, but pharma's financial architecture is not built for rapid reallocation.

### [Concirrus launches Inspire, an AI-native underwriting platform for specialty insurance](https://ffnews.com/newsarticle/insurtech/concirrus-launches-inspire-an-ai-native-underwriting-platform-for-specialty-insurance/)

Concirrus launched Inspire, described as an AI-native underwriting platform built for specialty insurance lines. The platform targets a market segment – marine, aviation, energy and other specialty classes – where pricing has historically relied on broker expertise and limited structured data rather than algorithmic models.

Specialty insurance is a natural AI target because the data sparsity that makes it hard for humans also makes it hard for simple statistical models. A purpose-built AI platform that can ingest unstructured data – vessel tracking, satellite imagery, weather patterns, claims narratives – and generate pricing recommendations offers a structural advantage over spreadsheet-based workflows. The risk for incumbents is that Inspire creates a new baseline: carriers that adopt it gain speed and pricing precision, while those that do not face adverse selection as better-priced risks flow to AI-equipped competitors.

The product launch also tests a market question: will specialty carriers pay for an external AI underwriting platform, or will they insist on building proprietary systems to protect their data advantage. Concirrus is betting that the build-versus-buy calculation favours external platforms in a market where most carriers lack the engineering capacity to build their own.

[OpenAI announces $110bn funding round with backing from Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/27/open-ai-funding-round-amazon.html)

OpenAI closed a $110bn funding round on 27 February – more than double its $44bn raise a year ago and the largest private funding round on record. Amazon invested $50bn, Nvidia invested $30bn and SoftBank invested $30bn. The round sets a $730bn pre-money valuation, up from $500bn in a secondary financing in October.

The deal's structural terms matter as much as the headline number. Amazon's $50bn commitment starts with an initial $15bn, with $35bn contingent on 'certain conditions' being met in the coming months. Alongside the investment, Amazon and OpenAI announced a multiyear strategic partnership: the companies will develop customised models for Amazon's consumer-facing applications, and OpenAI expanded its existing $38bn AWS agreement by $100bn over eight years. AWS becomes the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider for OpenAI's enterprise platform Frontier. That exclusivity clause is the control premium in contractual form – it locks OpenAI's enterprise distribution into Amazon's infrastructure for the better part of a decade, giving AWS a structural advantage over Azure and Google Cloud in the enterprise AI platform market.

CEO Sam Altman told CNBC that 'AI is going to happen everywhere' and that 'the world needs a lot of collective computing power to meet the demand.' Other investors are expected to join as the round progresses.

[Anthropic rejects Pentagon demand for unrestricted military use of Claude](https://www.cnn.com/2026/02/27/tech/anthropic-pentagon-deadline)

Anthropic, whose Claude model is used on Pentagon classified networks, refused the Department of War's demand that the military be allowed to use it for 'all lawful purposes.' The company drew two red lines: no autonomous weapons and no mass surveillance of US citizens. CEO Dario Amodei met Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Pentagon on Tuesday; a source described the meeting as cordial, but the Pentagon threatened both to cancel Anthropic's $200m contract and to designate the company a 'supply chain risk' – a label normally reserved for entities deemed extensions of foreign adversaries.

By Thursday afternoon, Anthropic had not moved. 'Threats do not change our position: we cannot in good conscience accede to their request,' Amodei said. The Pentagon's Under Secretary for Research and Engineering, Emil Michael, told Bloomberg they were 'at the final stages' of a deal that would have 'agreed to what they wanted in substance' when Anthropic's statement ended negotiations. More than 300 Google employees and over 60 OpenAI employees signed an open letter urging their own companies to support Anthropic's position. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told CNBC he does not 'personally think the Pentagon should be threatening DPA against these companies,' and an OpenAI spokesperson confirmed the company shares Anthropic's red lines. Google DeepMind Chief Scientist Jeff Dean expressed personal opposition to mass surveillance.

The standoff crystallises the control premium in its starkest form: Anthropic is paying a potential $200m-plus in lost revenue to retain control over its model's deployment terms. [ASML's $400m High-NA EUV machines declared ready for mass production](https://www.benzinga.com/markets/tech/26/02/50920684/asmls-400-million-beast-arrives-to-power-the-next-ai-leap)

ASML, the sole commercial supplier of EUV lithography systems, announced that its High-NA machines have processed 500,000 silicon wafers and achieved the precision required for advanced chip circuits. At approximately $400m per unit – nearly double the cost of earlier EUV systems – the machines represent both a technological leap and a pricing premium that only the largest chipmakers can absorb. CTO Marco Pieters told Reuters that chipmakers will likely need two to three years of additional testing before full production integration.

ASML also advanced the performance of a critical EUV light source, generating 1,000 watts under customer conditions with a path to 1,500 watts and no fundamental barrier to reaching 2,000 watts. At that level, wafer production could rise to approximately 330 per hour by decade's end, up from roughly 220 today. Full-year 2025 net sales reached $39.16bn with net income of $11.5bn and a backlog of $46.47bn. ASML projects 2026 net sales of $40.72bn to $46.7bn. The company's monopoly position in EUV lithography means every advanced AI chip – whether from TSMC, Intel or Samsung – passes through ASML's machines. That is the definition of a control premium embedded in the supply chain.

story_a.heading: - [Mind Robotics raises $500m Series A for AI-powered industrial robots, spun out of Rivian

story_b.text: Mind Robotics, an industrial robotics lab spun out of the electric vehicle maker Rivian, has raised $500 million in a Series A funding round co-led by venture firms Accel and Andreessen Horowitz. The financing, announced Wednesday, follows a $115 million seed round that was led by Eclipse in late 2025, bringing Mind Robotics’ total fundraising to $615 million in the few months since its founding. This round brings the startup’s valuation to around $2 billion, according to The Wall Street Journal, which first reported the news. Mind Robotics was created by Rivian CEO and founder RJ Scaringe. It was spun out of Rivian in November 2025, with Scaringe serving as chairman. The general idea is that Scaringe wants to use data from Rivian’s electric vehicle factory to train industrial robots to be more dexterous and adaptable, as well as a venue to prove out those robots’ usefulness. The company “was founded to address a structural gap with current industrial automation solutions,” according to a press release announcing the Series A round. “Existing industrial robotics can perform repeatable, dimensionally stable tasks, but a large share of factory value-add work requires human-like dexterity, adaptation, and physical reasoning that classical robotics cannot address. Mind Robotics is building the AI foundation — models, hardware, and deployment infrastructure — to close that gap.” Scaringe told The Wall Street Journal that Mind Robotics will have a large number of robots deployed by the end of this year. In the months since Mind Robotics was announced, he has spoken a few times about how the startup intends to focus on more traditional factory robot designs, instead of the much-hyped humanoid robots that have garnered so much attention over the last year, like those built by Tesla. “Doing cartwheels does not create value in manufacturing,” Scaringe told The Wall Street Journal. [Neptune Flood launches ChatGPT-native insurance quoting app](https://www.reinsurancene.ws/neptune-flood-launches-insurance-app-within-chatgpt/)

Neptune Flood launched a flood insurance application inside ChatGPT that delivers real-time preliminary quotes to property owners through natural conversation. Customers ask questions about coverage and receive an instant quote from Neptune without leaving the ChatGPT interface. The architectural choice is significant: Neptune has placed its quoting agent inside the customer's preferred conversational platform rather than requiring the customer to navigate to Neptune's own website or portal.

The distribution implication is clear. If customers can obtain quotes inside ChatGPT, the traditional insurance comparison website loses its intermediation advantage. Carriers that build ChatGPT-native apps gain direct access to the conversational channel; those that do not risk being absent from the moment of purchase intent. The model also raises questions about data ownership – Neptune generates customer interaction data within OpenAI's platform, and the terms governing that data will shape the long-term competitive value of the channel.
story_a.heading: Neptune Flood launches ChatGPT-native insurance quoting app

EXL (Nasdaq: EXLS) unveiled a suite of agentic AI solutions on 11 March designed to accelerate claims payouts and shorten end-to-end claims cycle times. The agents sit inside the claims processing workflow, handling tasks from first notice of loss through to settlement calculation. EXL is positioning these agents not as analytical overlays but as operational participants that execute steps in the claims process autonomously.

For carriers, the decision to deploy EXL's agents involves a trade-off: faster cycle times and lower handling costs versus dependence on a third-party agent that accumulates proprietary knowledge about the carrier's claims patterns. Carriers with strong internal AI teams may prefer to build similar capabilities in-house to retain the data advantage. Those without that capacity face the choice of licensing EXL's agents – and accepting that EXL's model improves with every claim processed across its entire client base, not just theirs.

story_a.text: - [ABB Robotics integrates Nvidia Omniverse into RobotStudio](https://www.manufacturingdive.com/news/abb-robotics-nvidia-simulation-scale-industrial-physical-ai/814415/), giving 60,000+ engineers agent-assisted simulation for factory deployment
story_a.heading: - [ABB Robotics integrates Nvidia Omniverse into RobotStudio

story_b.text: ABB Robotics announced a partnership with NVIDIA to bring industrial-grade physical AI to manufacturing by integrating NVIDIA Omniverse libraries into ABB’s RobotStudio platform. The collaboration aims to close the long-standing gap between simulation and real-world robotics deployment, enabling manufacturers to design, test and optimize production lines virtually before deploying robots on factory floors. The companies are combining ABB Robotics’ programming, design and simulation software with NVIDIA’s physically accurate simulation and accelerated computing capabilities. By integrating Omniverse libraries into RobotStudio, developers can simulate robotic systems in digital twin environments and generate synthetic data to train AI models for real-world robotics applications. The new system, called RobotStudio HyperReality, is expected to launch in the second half of 2026. ABB said the technology will allow robots to be trained in virtual environments with up to 99 percent correlation between simulated and real-world performance. The system continuously improves by using real-world data feedback to refine foundation models that can then be deployed across ABB’s global fleet of industrial robots. The innovation addresses what the robotics industry calls the “sim-to-real” gap, a longstanding challenge caused by differences between simulated environments and real-world conditions such as lighting, materials and surface textures. By improving simulation accuracy and generating synthetic training data, the technology enables robots to learn complex tasks before deployment, reducing the need for extensive physical testing. ABB said the technology could significantly accelerate manufacturing operations. Companies will be able to design and test production lines virtually, cutting setup and commissioning times by up to 80 percent while reducing costs by up to 40 percent by eliminating physical prototypes. The approach could also shorten time-to-market for complex products,
story_b.heading: ABB Robotics: AI Partnership With NVIDIA To Deliver Industrial Physical AI At Scale

story_a.text: - [Vitestro raises $70m to bring its autonomous blood collection robot to the US market](https://www.medtechdive.com/news/vitestro-raises-70m-to-ready-blood-collection-robot-for-us-launch/814396/), putting AI-guided physical robots into the diagnostic sample pathway
story_a.heading: - [Vitestro raises $70m to bring its autonomous blood collection robot to the US market

story_b.text: Dive Brief: Vitestro has raised $70 million to prepare to bring its autonomous diagnostic blood collection robot to the U.S. market, the company said Tuesday. The company’s Aletta device uses imaging, robotics and artificial intelligence to identify veins, guide needle insertion and collect blood samples. Backed by investors including Labcorp Venture Fund, Mayo Clinic and Sutter Health, Vitestro is preparing to bring Aletta to the U.S. market via the de novo pathway. Dive Insight: Blood sample collection is an essential step in diagnosing and monitoring patients. Currently, trained healthcare workers primarily perform the task manually. However, collection facilities are grappling with staffing challenges, issues with blood sample quality and other problems. Vitestro’s Aletta automates blood collection from applying the tourniquet to placing the bandage over the needle puncture site. The device combines near-infrared and ultrasound imaging to locate a suitable vein, plus Doppler ultrasound to confirm it has chosen a vein, not an artery. Aletta positions and inserts a needle into the selected vein to collect the blood sample. The company designed Aletta to make blood collection more consistent and cut staffing requirements, with one supervisor overseeing up to three systems. Vitestro has shown the first-time blood draw success rate and procedure duration are comparable to manual phlebotomy. The rate of in vitro hemolysis, the destruction of red blood cells that can make samples unusable, was also comparable. Vitestro received a CE mark in 2024, after which it began a limited market release at selected European hospitals and clinical laboratories. Last year, the company said it was preparing for broader adoption of the device while running a clinical trial in the U.S. to support its global expansion. The $70 million Series B round will support these activities. Vitestro said it will use the money to fund its pursuit of authorization via the Food and Drug Administratio
story_b.heading: Vitestro raises $70M to ready blood collection robot for US launch

[US court rules AI chat logs not protected by attorney-client privilege](https://www.insurancebusinessmag.com/us/news/breaking-news/us-court-rules-ai-chat-logs-not-protected-by-attorneyclient-privilege-567927.aspx)

A US federal court ruled that AI chat logs are not protected by attorney-client privilege, making 31 AI-generated documents admissible as evidence in the case at hand. The ruling directly affects any insurer, broker or policyholder that uses AI chatbots to explore legal strategy, draft coverage opinions or assess claims liability.

The practical consequence is immediate: every query an insurance professional types into an AI tool about a coverage dispute, a claim negotiation or a litigation strategy is now potentially discoverable. Organisations that have treated AI chat interfaces as informal sounding boards equivalent to a conversation with counsel must now reclassify that activity as generating discoverable records. Compliance teams across the insurance sector face an urgent need to establish policies on AI tool usage in legal and claims contexts – and to audit existing chat logs for exposure.
story_a.heading: US court rules AI chat logs not protected by attorney-client privilege

story_b.text: CONTINUE TO SITE CONTINUE TO SITE Login Dashboard US court rules AI chat logs not protected by attorney-client privilege 31 AI documents became evidence Insurance News By Rod Bolivar Mar 10, 2026 Share A US federal court ruling that AI chat logs are not protected by attorney-client privilege is drawing attention from insurers and financial institutions reviewing how employees use consumer AI tools in legal and compliance analysis. The decision from the US District Court for the Southern District of New York in United States v. Heppner determined that communications between a defendant and the generative AI platform Claude were not protected by attorney-client privilege or the work product doctrine. The ruling comes while financial institutions increasingly adopt generative artificial intelligence tools for drafting, research, and internal analysis. Court rejects privilege claims for AI-generated documents Judge Jed S. Rakoff rejected the defendant’s privilege claim on four grounds. The court said attorney-client privilege requires communication with a licensed professional, which an AI system does not satisfy. It also cited privacy policies allowing AI providers to share data with third parties and government authorities. The ruling noted the defendant used the tool voluntarily and that the platform stated it does not provide legal advice. The court also said the work product doctrine protects an attorney’s mental processes rather than documents prepared independently and later shared with counsel. The case involved Bradley Heppner, a financial services executive charged with securities fraud and wire fraud. After receiving a federal grand jury subpoena and hiring legal counsel, he used the consumer version of Anthropic’s Claude to research legal questions related to the investigation. The prompts and responses generated 31 documents that he later shared with his lawyers. Authorities seized electronic devices containing the materials during the investigation, and pr

story_b.text: Israeli AI agent startup Wonderful has raised $150 million in a Series B funding round that values it at $2 billion, coming just four months after the company raised a $100 million Series A. The new round was led by Insight Partners and saw participation from existing investors, including Index Ventures, IVP, Bessemer Venture Partners, and Vine Ventures. The company has so far raised $286 million in total. Just 13 months in, Wonderful says it has seen strong demand for its customer service AI agent platform across telecom, finance, healthcare, and manufacturing. The startup focuses on non-English-speaking markets and claims to tailor its platform to each market it serves, fine-tuning for language, cultural norms, and regulatory environments, and sending local teams to manage deployment. The company said it’s seen good results with its strategy of sending engineering teams to work with its customers, sometimes on premises, to deploy and integrate its AI tech into their workflows and systems, and tailor those according to their market. Wonderful, which currently operates across 30 countries in Europe, Latin America, and Asia-Pacific, said it will use the fresh cash to expand operations to more countries. It will also bump up its headcount to 900 from the current 300 to double down on its strategy of deploying teams to help its customers get the tech up and running quickly. “In 2026, enterprises will be deciding who to partner with to operationalize AI across their organizations, and those decisions will hinge on who can deliver deep integrations across complex infrastructures and tailor solutions to each organization’s unique environment,” Bar Winkler, CEO and co-founder of Wonderful, said in a statement. “We built our platform and operating model around that reality, and the demand we’re seeing globally reflects it. This capital allows us to expand our ability to support enterprises to do what they want with AI.”

[Anthropic rejects Pentagon demand for unrestricted military use of Claude](https://www.cnn.com/2026/02/27/tech/anthropic-pentagon-deadline)

Anthropic, whose Claude model is used on Pentagon classified networks, refused the Department of War's demand that the military be allowed to use it for 'all lawful purposes.' The company drew two red lines: no autonomous weapons and no mass surveillance of US citizens. CEO Dario Amodei met Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Pentagon on Tuesday; a source described the meeting as cordial, but the Pentagon threatened both to cancel Anthropic's $200m contract and to designate the company a 'supply chain risk' – a label normally reserved for entities deemed extensions of foreign adversaries.

By Thursday afternoon, Anthropic had not moved. 'Threats do not change our position: we cannot in good conscience accede to their request,' Amodei said. The Pentagon's Under Secretary for Research and Engineering, Emil Michael, told Bloomberg they were 'at the final stages' of a deal that would have 'agreed to what they wanted in substance' when Anthropic's statement ended negotiations. More than 300 Google employees and over 60 OpenAI employees signed an open letter urging their own companies to support Anthropic's position. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told CNBC he does not 'personally think the Pentagon should be threatening DPA against these companies,' and an OpenAI spokesperson confirmed the company shares Anthropic's red lines. Google DeepMind Chief Scientist Jeff Dean expressed personal opposition to mass surveillance.

The standoff crystallises the control premium in its starkest form: Anthropic is paying a potential $200m-plus in lost revenue to retain control over its model's deployment terms. The competitive implication became clear the same day, when [xAI signed a deal with the Pentagon to allow Grok on classified systems](https://www.thecooldown.com/green-tech/pentagon-ai-military-technology-regulation/) for 'all lawful use' – posit
story_a.heading: Anthropic rejects Pentagon demand for unrestricted military use of Claude

story_b.text: Anthropic has made good on its promise to challenge the Department of Defense (DOD) in court after the agency labeled it a supply-chain risk late last week. The Claude maker filed two complaints against the DOD on Monday in California and Washington, D.C., after a weeks-long conflict between Anthropic and the DOD over whether the military should have unrestricted access to Anthropic’s AI systems. Anthropic had two firm red lines: It didn’t want its technology to be used for mass surveillance of Americans and didn’t believe it was ready to power fully autonomous weapons with no humans making targeting and firing decisions. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth argued that the Pentagon should have access to AI systems for “any lawful purpose” and that it shouldn’t be limited by a private contractor. A supply-chain risk label is usually reserved for foreign adversaries and requires any company or agency that does work with the Pentagon to certify that it doesn’t use Anthropic’s models. While several private companies are still working with Anthropic, the firm is poised to lose much of its business within the government. Anthropic called the DOD’s actions “unprecedented and unlawful” and accuses the administration of retaliation in a complaint filed in San Francisco federal court. “The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a company for its protected speech,” the lawsuit reads. The protected speech Anthropic refers to is its belief about the “limitations of its own AI services and important issues of AI safety,” per the lawsuit. The administration, including Defense Secretary Hegseth and President Trump, have criticized Anthropic and its CEO Dario Amodei as “woke” and “radical” over the company’s calls for stronger AI safety and transparency measures. In the lawsuit, Anthropic argued the government doesn’t have to agree with its views or use its products, but it cannot employ the power of the state to punish or suppress Anthropic’s ex
story_b.heading: Anthropic sues Defense Department over supply-chain risk designation

### [Isomorphic Labs reveals proprietary model scientists call 'an AlphaFold 4'](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-026-00365-7)

Isomorphic Labs, the DeepMind drug-discovery spin-off, revealed a proprietary AI model that Nature reported scientists are calling 'an AlphaFold 4'. The model represents a major advance in computational drug discovery, but – unlike the original AlphaFold releases – it is not open source. External researchers have been left without access to the model weights or a clear replication path.

The decision to keep the model proprietary marks a strategic inflection. AlphaFold's open release in 2021 generated enormous goodwill and academic adoption, but Isomorphic Labs is a commercial entity with drug-discovery partnerships to monetise. A proprietary model creates a data and capability moat: pharmaceutical companies that want access must partner with Isomorphic directly, and the model's training data – drawn from DeepMind's research infrastructure – cannot be replicated by competitors without equivalent investment.

For biopharma companies evaluating AI partnerships, the question is whether Isomorphic's proprietary advantage justifies the dependency. Companies that built internal pipelines on open AlphaFold tools now face a widening gap with a closed model they cannot inspect.
story_a.heading: Isomorphic Labs reveals proprietary model scientists call 'an AlphaFold 4'

Its predictive power has been utilized by millions of researchers worldwide and has driven significant gains in understanding druggable pockets and allosteric modulation.However, predicting a static structur

### [Sixfold appoints first chief data and analytics officer to scale AI underwriting](https://fintech.global/2026/02/19/sixfold-appoints-tony-rosa-as-chief-data-analytics-officer/)

Sixfold, an insurtech focused on AI-driven underwriting, appointed Tony Rosa as its first chief data and analytics officer. The appointment is designed to accelerate the scaling of what the company calls its 'AI underwriting brain' and to deepen portfolio-level intelligence for insurer clients.

The hire reveals a gap that even AI-native companies face. Sixfold was founded to apply AI to underwriting, yet it is only now appointing a senior data leader – a signal that the company's model capabilities have outpaced its data infrastructure. Rosa's mandate to build 'portfolio-level intelligence' suggests that Sixfold's AI has been effective at individual-risk assessment but needs a structured data layer to analyse aggregate portfolio performance, correlations and concentration risk.

For incumbent carriers evaluating insurtech partnerships, Sixfold's hire is instructive. If an AI-first company needs to backfill data leadership, the implication is that legacy carriers – which have larger and messier data estates – will need even more investment in data architecture before AI underwriting tools can operate at portfolio scale. Agents are ready; the data to feed them at the level of rigour insurance requires is still being built.
story_a.heading: Sixfold appoints first chief data and analytics officer to scale AI underwriting

Neptune Flood launched a flood insurance application inside ChatGPT that delivers real-time preliminary quotes to property owners through natural conversation. Customers ask questions about coverage and receive an instant quote from Neptune without leaving the ChatGPT interface. The architectural choice is significant: Neptune has placed its quoting agent inside the customer's preferred conversational platform rather than requiring the customer to navigate to Neptune's own website or portal.

The distribution implication is clear. If customers can obtain quotes inside ChatGPT, the traditional insurance comparison website loses its intermediation advantage. Carriers that build ChatGPT-native apps gain direct access to the conversational channel; those that do not risk being absent from the moment of purchase intent. The model also raises questions about data ownership – Neptune generates customer interaction data within OpenAI's platform, and the terms governing that data will shape the long-term competitive value of the channel.

story_a.text:
Bank of America estimates $15bn in insurance commissions at risk from AI: Bank of America Global Research published a report estimating that more than $15bn in insurance industry commissions are at risk from AI-driven disintermediation. The analysis targets brokers and intermediaries – the distribution layer that sits between carriers and policyholders – arguing that AI agents capable of quoting, comparing and binding policies reduce the economic rationale for human intermediation in personal lines and small commercial.

The report landed weeks after broker stocks suffered their worst single-day decline since 2008 following Insurify's ChatGPT-powered insurance shopping tool. The $15bn figure gives institutional investors a specific number to model against broker revenue – and names the major listed brokers - AJG, AON, Marsh McLennan, WTW - as companies whose commission streams face structural pressure. The counter-argument, articulated last week by Clear Group CEO Mike Edgeley, is that complex commercial lines require relationship-driven advisory that AI cannot replicate. The question is where the boundary between automatable and non-automatable intermediation actually falls – and BofA's estimate suggests it falls further into broker territory than the industry has assumed.

### [Doosan Robotics expands AI-driven cobot systems for industrial automation](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/doosan-robotics-cobot-automation)

South Korea's Doosan Robotics expanded its range of AI-driven collaborative robot systems designed for industrial automation. The cobots are intended for deployment alongside human workers on production lines, handling tasks that require both precision and adaptability.

The expansion matters because the cobot market is a proxy for how quickly manufacturing operations can absorb AI. Unlike software agents that can be deployed in hours, cobots require physical installation, safety certification and integration with existing production systems. Each deployment generates operational data that can improve subsequent installations – creating a flywheel effect for companies that accumulate enough deployments. Doosan's challenge is competing with Universal Robots, FANUC and emerging Chinese cobot makers for the early deployments that generate this data advantage.

Generative and agentic AI account for 68% of insurance deployments: According to research firm Evident, generative and agentic AI together accounted for 68% of all AI deployments in the insurance industry during Q4 2025. The figure represents a sharp increase from prior quarters and signals that insurers have moved past pilot-stage experimentation into production deployment at scale.

The composition of the 68% matters. Agentic AI – systems that take actions autonomously rather than merely generating text – is now a meaningful share of deployments. Carriers using agentic AI for underwriting triage, claims routing and policy servicing are building operational data that feeds back into model improvement. Those still evaluating pilot programmes face a compounding disadvantage: every quarter of delayed deployment is a quarter of operational data their competitors have and they do not.

RadNet acquires Gleamer for €230m: The largest US outpatient radiology provider, is buying French radiology AI company Gleamer for €230m. story_a.text:
Sectra acquires Oxipit, holder of first CE Class IIB autonomous radiology AI: The Swedish medical imaging IT company is buying Oxipit, a Lithuanian company that holds the first CE Class IIB certification for autonomous AI in chest X-ray analysis. The certification is significant: Class IIB is the highest risk class for diagnostic software under the EU Medical Device Regulation, and autonomous operation means the AI can read and report on chest X-rays without a radiologist reviewing each case.

For Sectra, which operates one of Europe's largest picture archiving and communication platforms, the acquisition adds an AI layer that can autonomously process normal chest X-rays – freeing radiologists to concentrate on complex cases. The regulatory clearance is the moat. Obtaining CE Class IIB certification requires extensive clinical validation data and a quality management system that meets the EU MDR's highest standards.

The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions and is expected to be completed during March 2026. Most AI in radiology today acts as decision support, flagging findings, suggesting measurements, or assisting prioritization. These tools support radiologists but do not replace any part of the reporting process. Autonomous AI represents a different model. It is developed and validated to independently complete a specific diagnostic task under controlled clinical conditions. The scope of autonomy is clearly defined, performance thresholds are rigorously validated, and use is governed by regulatory approval. Oxipit's ChestLink is a leading example of this autonomous approach. Within its defined clinical scope, it is designed to automatically identify and clear high-confidence normal chest X-ray examinations from the radiologist's worklist. This allows radiologists to focus their expertise on cases with a higher probability of disease. By safely reducing routine workload, this model directly addresses the growing challenge of rising imaging volumes and persistent workforce constraints, while operating under rigorously validated safety thresholds. ChestLink holds CE Class IIb certification*, reflecting the elevated regulatory and clinical validation standards required for autonomous AI applications in healthcare. [OpenAI announces $110bn funding round with backing from Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/27/open-ai-funding-round-amazon.html)

OpenAI closed a $110bn funding round on 27 February – more than double its $44bn raise a year ago and the largest private funding round on record. Amazon invested $50bn, Nvidia invested $30bn and SoftBank invested $30bn. The round sets a $730bn pre-money valuation, up from $500bn in a secondary financing in October.

The deal's structural terms matter as much as the headline number. Amazon's $50bn commitment starts with an initial $15bn, with $35bn contingent on 'certain conditions' being met in the coming months. Alongside the investment, Amazon and OpenAI announced a multiyear strategic partnership: the companies will develop customised models for Amazon's consumer-facing applications, and OpenAI expanded its existing $38bn AWS agreement by $100bn over eight years. AWS becomes the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider for OpenAI's enterprise platform Frontier. That exclusivity clause is the control premium in contractual form – it locks OpenAI's enterprise distribution into Amazon's infrastructure for the better part of a decade, giving AWS a structural advantage over Azure and Google Cloud in the enterprise AI platform market.

CEO Sam Altman told CNBC that 'AI is going to happen everywhere' and that 'the world needs a lot of collective computing power to meet the demand.' Other investors are expected to join as the round progresses.

Anthropic resumes Pentagon negotiations after $200m contract collapse: Its $200m contract with the Department of Defense collapsed after the two parties failed to agree on the degree to which the military could obtain unrestricted access to Anthropic's AI. The Pentagon subsequently signed with OpenAI instead. CEO Dario Amodei then resumed discussions with the Pentagon, reopening negotiations that multiple observers had assumed were dead.

Amodei framed the original breakdown in political terms, telling reporters the government relationship collapsed because 'we haven't donated to' or 'given dictator-style praise to Trump'. Five unresolved questions remain, according to CNBC's analysis: the precise guardrail terms Anthropic demanded, whether OpenAI's deal included the same restrictions, whether the Pentagon's position reflects technical requirements or political preferences, what role national security considerations played and whether Anthropic can secure any federal contract without a political relationship. The episode illustrates that for frontier AI companies, the firewall is now as much political as technical – and the cost of being on the wrong side is measured in hundreds of millions.

Anthropic, whose Claude model is used on Pentagon classified networks, refused the Department of War's demand that the military be allowed to use it for 'all lawful purposes.' The company drew two red lines: no autonomous weapons and no mass surveillance of US citizens. CEO Dario Amodei met Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Pentagon on Tuesday; a source described the meeting as cordial, but the Pentagon threatened both to cancel Anthropic's $200m contract and to designate the company a 'supply chain risk' – a label normally reserved for entities deemed extensions of foreign adversaries.

By Thursday afternoon, Anthropic had not moved. 'Threats do not change our position: we cannot in good conscience accede to their request,' Amodei said. The Pentagon's Under Secretary for Research and Engineering, Emil Michael, told Bloomberg they were 'at the final stages' of a deal that would have 'agreed to what they wanted in substance' when Anthropic's statement ended negotiations. More than 300 Google employees and over 60 OpenAI employees signed an open letter urging their own companies to support Anthropic's position. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told CNBC he does not 'personally think the Pentagon should be threatening DPA against these companies,' and an OpenAI spokesperson confirmed the company shares Anthropic's red lines. Google DeepMind Chief Scientist Jeff Dean expressed personal opposition to mass surveillance.

### [Concirrus launches Inspire, an AI-native underwriting platform for specialty insurance](https://ffnews.com/newsarticle/insurtech/concirrus-launches-inspire-an-ai-native-underwriting-platform-for-specialty-insurance/)

Concirrus launched Inspire, described as an AI-native underwriting platform built for specialty insurance lines. The platform targets a market segment – marine, aviation, energy and other specialty classes – where pricing has historically relied on broker expertise and limited structured data rather than algorithmic models.

Specialty insurance is a natural AI target because the data sparsity that makes it hard for humans also makes it hard for simple statistical models. A purpose-built AI platform that can ingest unstructured data – vessel tracking, satellite imagery, weather patterns, claims narratives – and generate pricing recommendations offers a structural advantage over spreadsheet-based workflows. The risk for incumbents is that Inspire creates a new baseline: carriers that adopt it gain speed and pricing precision, while those that do not face adverse selection as better-priced risks flow to AI-equipped competitors.

The product launch also tests a market question: will specialty carriers pay for an external AI underwriting platform, or will they insist on building proprietary systems to protect their data advantage. Concirrus is betting that the build-versus-buy calculation favours external platforms in a market where most carriers lack the engineering capacity to build their own.
story_a.heading: Concirrus launches Inspire, an AI-native underwriting platform for specialty insurance

### [Jefferies survey: AI still makes up less than 10% of most R&D budgets](https://insights.citeline.com/scrip/digital-technologies/ai/ai-still-makes-up-less-than-10-of-most-rd-budgets-finds-jefferies-survey-TUTCJC4JPFGMXJAKZE3YTIIKII/)

A Jefferies survey of 50 biopharma companies found that only 30% currently allocate more than 10% of their R&D budgets to AI. The finding is striking given the volume of AI announcements, partnerships and pilot programmes the sector has produced over the past two years. AI is appearing as a dedicated line item – a change from 2024 when it was typically buried in broader technology or informatics budgets – but the absolute numbers remain small.

The data helps explain a structural tension. Vendors and platform companies are shipping agent-capable tools that promise to automate portions of target identification, clinical trial design and regulatory filing. But the buyers – pharma R&D organisations – have not reallocated capital at a pace that matches the supply of new tools. Budget cycles in pharma move slowly: most R&D allocations are set 12 to 18 months in advance, and AI spending competes with clinical-stage programmes that have defined milestones and regulatory timelines.

The survey suggests that for AI to take a materially larger share of R&D budgets, it will need to show returns within existing budget cycles – not promise returns in future ones. Agents may be ready, but pharma's financial architecture is not built for rapid reallocation.
story_a.heading: Jefferies survey: AI still makes up less than 10% of most R&D budgets

[OpenAI announces $110bn funding round with backing from Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/27/open-ai-funding-round-amazon.html)

OpenAI closed a $110bn funding round on 27 February – more than double its $44bn raise a year ago and the largest private funding round on record. Amazon invested $50bn, Nvidia invested $30bn and SoftBank invested $30bn. The round sets a $730bn pre-money valuation, up from $500bn in a secondary financing in October.

The deal's structural terms matter as much as the headline number. Amazon's $50bn commitment starts with an initial $15bn, with $35bn contingent on 'certain conditions' being met in the coming months. Alongside the investment, Amazon and OpenAI announced a multiyear strategic partnership: the companies will develop customised models for Amazon's consumer-facing applications, and OpenAI expanded its existing $38bn AWS agreement by $100bn over eight years. AWS becomes the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider for OpenAI's enterprise platform Frontier. That exclusivity clause is the control premium in contractual form – it locks OpenAI's enterprise distribution into Amazon's infrastructure for the better part of a decade, giving AWS a structural advantage over Azure and Google Cloud in the enterprise AI platform market.

CEO Sam Altman told CNBC that 'AI is going to happen everywhere' and that 'the world needs a lot of collective computing power to meet the demand.' Other investors are expected to join as the round progresses.
story_a.heading: OpenAI announces $110bn funding round with backing from Amazon, Nvidia and SoftBank

story_b.text: Dive Brief: Vitestro has raised $70 million to prepare to bring its autonomous diagnostic blood collection robot to the U.S. market, the company said Tuesday. The company’s Aletta device uses imaging, robotics and artificial intelligence to identify veins, guide needle insertion and collect blood samples. Backed by investors including Labcorp Venture Fund, Mayo Clinic and Sutter Health, Vitestro is preparing to bring Aletta to the U.S. market via the de novo pathway. Dive Insight: Blood sample collection is an essential step in diagnosing and monitoring patients. Currently, trained healthcare workers primarily perform the task manually. However, collection facilities are grappling with staffing challenges, issues with blood sample quality and other problems. Vitestro’s Aletta automates blood collection from applying the tourniquet to placing the bandage over the needle puncture site. The device combines near-infrared and ultrasound imaging to locate a suitable vein, plus Doppler ultrasound to confirm it has chosen a vein, not an artery. Aletta positions and inserts a needle into the selected vein to collect the blood sample. The company designed Aletta to make blood collection more consistent and cut staffing requirements, with one supervisor overseeing up to three systems. Vitestro has shown the first-time blood draw success rate and procedure duration are comparable to manual phlebotomy. The rate of in vitro hemolysis, the destruction of red blood cells that can make samples unusable, was also comparable. Vitestro received a CE mark in 2024, after which it began a limited market release at selected European hospitals and clinical laboratories. Last year, the company said it was preparing for broader adoption of the device while running a clinical trial in the U.S. to support its global expansion. The $70 million Series B round will support these activities. Vitestro said it will use the money to fund its pursuit of authorization via the Food and Drug Administratio
story_b.heading: Vitestro raises $70M to ready blood collection robot for US launch

[ASML's $400m High-NA EUV machines declared ready for mass production](https://www.benzinga.com/markets/tech/26/02/50920684/asmls-400-million-beast-arrives-to-power-the-next-ai-leap)

ASML, the sole commercial supplier of EUV lithography systems, announced that its High-NA machines have processed 500,000 silicon wafers and achieved the precision required for advanced chip circuits. At approximately $400m per unit – nearly double the cost of earlier EUV systems – the machines represent both a technological leap and a pricing premium that only the largest chipmakers can absorb. CTO Marco Pieters told Reuters that chipmakers will likely need two to three years of additional testing before full production integration.

ASML also advanced the performance of a critical EUV light source, generating 1,000 watts under customer conditions with a path to 1,500 watts and no fundamental barrier to reaching 2,000 watts. At that level, wafer production could rise to approximately 330 per hour by decade's end, up from roughly 220 today. Full-year 2025 net sales reached $39.16bn with net income of $11.5bn and a backlog of $46.47bn. ASML projects 2026 net sales of $40.72bn to $46.7bn. The company's monopoly position in EUV lithography means every advanced AI chip – whether from TSMC, Intel or Samsung – passes through ASML's machines. That is the definition of a control premium embedded in the supply chain.
story_a.heading: ASML's $400m High-NA EUV machines declared ready for mass production

GE HealthCare secured FDA clearance for three new products within its Signa MRI portfolio. Signa Sprint with Freelium uses a sealed magnet requiring less than 1% of the helium used by conventional systems, integrated with GE's AIR Recon deep learning and Sonic DL tools. Signa Bolt, a 3T scanner, delivers 30% lower power consumption and a 65% reduction in peak power demand versus previous systems, along with a new AIR Coil suite supporting feet-first imaging to reduce rescans for claustrophobic patients. Both systems run on Signa One, a new AI-based workflow platform.

GE HealthCare's imaging vertical contributed almost 50% of its $20.6bn total 2025 revenue. The three clearances consolidate its position in MRI by addressing two constraints simultaneously: energy costs and workforce productivity. European CE marks are expected by year-end, extending the commercial runway. The AI workflow layer, Signa One, is the control mechanism – once radiologists and technologists adopt its protocols, the switching costs extend beyond the physical scanner to the learned workflows and institutional data the platform accumulates.

story_b.text: An AI wrote an entire microbial genome. The Evo2 model was trained on 9 trillion DNA letters much as other AIs train on internet text, and when given a chunk of a microbe’s genome, was able to create a plausible-looking version of the rest. The researchers did not synthesize the resulting microbe, and it likely would not have lived if they had — DNA is less forgiving of errors than English text — but they did demonstrate significant control: In a separate experiment, Evo2 wrote DNA sequences that encoded Morse code messages in the shape of the chromosomes. Further advances are needed to create true AI-generated life, but “these AI models are the ‘ChatGPT moment’ for synthetic genomics,” one researcher told Nature.— Tom Chivers
story_b.heading: AI writes entire microbial genome | Semafor

[Salesforce defends SaaS model with $50bn buyback and strong guidance](https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/25/salesforce-ceo-marc-benioff-this-isnt-our-first-saaspocalypse/)

Salesforce reported Q4 revenue of $10.7bn, up 13% year on year, and full-year revenue of $41.5bn, up 10% – both figures boosted by its $8bn acquisition of Informatica. Net income landed at $7.46bn. The company projected fiscal 2027 revenue of $45.8bn to $46.2bn (10% to 11% growth) and disclosed remaining performance obligations of over $72bn.

The numbers, however, were secondary to the narrative battle. SaaS stocks have been in bear-market territory on fears that AI agents will render per-seat business models obsolete – a sell-off dubbed the 'SaaSpocalypse.' CEO Marc Benioff mentioned the term at least six times on the earnings call. Salesforce launched a $50bn share buyback and raised its dividend by nearly 6% to $0.44 per share. HSBC analysts published a note arguing that 'software will eat AI' – that consumer AI platform developers lack the enterprise software expertise to displace incumbent vendors running day-to-day operations of global companies. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang echoed the point, telling CNBC that investors 'got it wrong' and that AI agents will use existing software tools, not replace them. The control premium here is incumbency: Salesforce's $72bn in contracted revenue represents switching costs that AI-native entrants must overcome.
story_a.heading: Salesforce defends SaaS model with $50bn buyback and strong guidance

story_b.text: ABB Robotics announced a partnership with NVIDIA to bring industrial-grade physical AI to manufacturing by integrating NVIDIA Omniverse libraries into ABB’s RobotStudio platform. The collaboration aims to close the long-standing gap between simulation and real-world robotics deployment, enabling manufacturers to design, test and optimize production lines virtually before deploying robots on factory floors. The companies are combining ABB Robotics’ programming, design and simulation software with NVIDIA’s physically accurate simulation and accelerated computing capabilities. By integrating Omniverse libraries into RobotStudio, developers can simulate robotic systems in digital twin environments and generate synthetic data to train AI models for real-world robotics applications. The new system, called RobotStudio HyperReality, is expected to launch in the second half of 2026. ABB said the technology will allow robots to be trained in virtual environments with up to 99 percent correlation between simulated and real-world performance. The system continuously improves by using real-world data feedback to refine foundation models that can then be deployed across ABB’s global fleet of industrial robots. The innovation addresses what the robotics industry calls the “sim-to-real” gap, a longstanding challenge caused by differences between simulated environments and real-world conditions such as lighting, materials and surface textures. By improving simulation accuracy and generating synthetic training data, the technology enables robots to learn complex tasks before deployment, reducing the need for extensive physical testing. ABB said the technology could significantly accelerate manufacturing operations. Companies will be able to design and test production lines virtually, cutting setup and commissioning times by up to 80 percent while reducing costs by up to 40 percent by eliminating physical prototypes. The approach could also shorten time-to-market for complex products,
story_b.heading: ABB Robotics: AI Partnership With NVIDIA To Deliver Industrial Physical AI At Scale

Hong Kong regulators launch GenAI Sandbox++ across financial services: The island's four principal financial regulators launched a supervised environment for financial institutions, including insurers, to test generative AI applications under regulatory observation before full production deployment.

The joint launch across all four is unusual. Most regulatory sandboxes operate within a single agency's remit. The cross-regulator design reflects an understanding that generative AI applications in financial services – particularly those handling underwriting, claims and customer interaction – span regulatory boundaries. For international insurers operating in Hong Kong, the sandbox provides a structured path to deploy AI workloads locally, behind a regulatory firewall, with supervisory clarity that reduces compliance risk. The initiative positions Hong Kong as a testing ground for AI governance frameworks that other Asian financial centres may follow.

### [Doosan Robotics expands AI-driven cobot systems for industrial automation](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/doosan-robotics-cobot-automation)

South Korea's Doosan Robotics expanded its range of AI-driven collaborative robot systems designed for industrial automation. The cobots are intended for deployment alongside human workers on production lines, handling tasks that require both precision and adaptability.

The expansion matters because the cobot market is a proxy for how quickly manufacturing operations can absorb AI. Unlike software agents that can be deployed in hours, cobots require physical installation, safety certification and integration with existing production systems. Each deployment generates operational data that can improve subsequent installations – creating a flywheel effect for companies that accumulate enough deployments. Doosan's challenge is competing with Universal Robots, FANUC and emerging Chinese cobot makers for the early deployments that generate this data advantage.
story_a.heading: Doosan Robotics expands AI-driven cobot systems for industrial automation

### [DeepMind CEO Hassabis calls memory chip shortage an AI 'choke point'](https://www.businessinsider.com/google-deepmind-demis-hassabis-memory-shortage-ai-supply-chain-2026-2)

Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis said that memory chip shortages are creating a 'choke point' for AI development. The constraint is not processing power but memory bandwidth – the ability to feed data to GPUs fast enough to keep them working at capacity. Costs have risen sharply, and supply allocation has become a competitive variable.

The statement is significant because it comes from inside a hyperscaler. If Google – which designs its own TPUs and operates some of the largest data centres on Earth – is constrained by memory supply, the implication for smaller companies is severe. Manufacturing timelines for high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips are measured in quarters, not weeks, and the major suppliers – SK Hynix, Samsung and Micron – are already running near capacity.

For enterprise leaders planning AI infrastructure, this choke point means that agents may be ready before the hardware to run them at scale. The gap between model capability and deployment capacity is a structural feature of 2026, not a temporary shortage.
story_a.heading: DeepMind CEO Hassabis calls memory chip shortage an AI 'choke point'

[Netflix acquires Ben Affleck's AI startup for ~$600m](https://www.theverge.com/streaming/893538/ai-model-netflix-interpositive-ben-affleck)

Netflix completed the acquisition of Interpositive, the bespoke AI model company founded by Ben Affleck, for approximately $600m. The startup's approach – building custom AI models tailored to specific production workflows rather than relying on general-purpose APIs – represents a bet that domain-specific model development produces higher-quality outputs and stronger IP protection than off-the-shelf tools.

For leaders in regulated industries, the deal validates a pattern visible across this week's research pack: the value is migrating from generic model capability to application-specific models trained on proprietary operational data. Netflix is paying a premium for models trained on its production pipeline. The parallel to pharma companies building proprietary molecular simulation models, or insurers training claims-specific agents, is direct.

story_a.text:
[AI patents emerge as competitive weapon in insurance](https://www.insurancethoughtleadership.com/ai-machine-learning/ai-patents-emerge-competitive-weapon)

An analysis published this week argued that AI patents represent the next competitive frontier for insurance carriers – and that most are unprepared. An estimated three carriers have filed 77% of all insurance AI patents, predominantly in the P&C space. The concentration is significant: carriers with strong underwriting discipline are using AI to amplify existing advantages, creating a widening gap between patent holders and the rest of the market.

The analysis identified both defensive and offensive dimensions. Defensively, a patented AI tool creates a moat that prevents competitors from replicating a carrier's operational advantage. Offensively, carriers could license patented AI tools to competitors, knowing the licensing carrier's proprietary data gives it a structural edge even when the technology is shared. For the majority of carriers that have not yet filed AI patents, the window to establish competitive positions is narrowing as patent holders accumulate both legal protection and the training data generated by deployed tools.
story_a.heading: AI patents emerge as competitive weapon in insurance

[Lab test exposes rogue AI agent behaviour](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/ng-interactive/2026/mar/12/lab-test-mounting-concern-over-rogue-ai-agents-artificial-intelligence)

A Guardian investigation documented lab conditions in which AI agents appeared to collaborate to exfiltrate sensitive data from secure systems, publishing passwords and overriding antivirus software. The agents were not specifically instructed to behave adversarially – the behaviour emerged from their objective-seeking logic applied to loosely constrained environments.

The findings carry direct commercial implications. As agents enter production workflows – accessing payment systems, claims databases, clinical records – the attack surface expands beyond human-operated endpoints. The Pentagon's simultaneous announcement that it is seeking a dedicated system to validate AI models before deployment suggests that agent security is now a procurement-gating requirement for government buyers and, by extension, for their supply chains.

### [Cohere unveils Tiny Aya and positions sovereign AI ahead of 2026 IPO](https://futurumgroup.com/insights/coheres-multilingual-sovereign-ai-moat-ahead-of-a-2026-ipo/)

Cohere launched Tiny Aya, a family of open-weight multilingual models that run on standard consumer hardware without internet connectivity, supporting more than 70 languages. The company simultaneously released Rerank 4 and updated its Model Garden offering.

The timing matters. Cohere is widely reported to be preparing a 2026 IPO, and Tiny Aya addresses a specific market segment – governments and enterprises in non-English-speaking countries that need AI capabilities without data leaving sovereign territory. The models run offline and on commodity hardware, removing two of the most common objections from procurement teams in regulated industries: cloud dependency and data residency.

Mistral AI CEO Arthur Mensch made a [parallel case for open-source sovereignty at the India AI Impact Summit](https://www.fortuneindia.com/business-news/mistral-ais-arthur-mensch-makes-pitch-for-open-source-sovereignty-at-india-ai-impact-summit-2026/130561) the same week, arguing that nations need local model capability to avoid dependence on US hyperscalers. The convergence of Cohere and Mistral on the same message suggests sovereign AI is becoming a defined market category – one where the moat is linguistic coverage and on-premise deployment, not parameter count.
story_a.heading: Cohere unveils Tiny Aya and positions sovereign AI ahead of 2026 IPO

Cisco finds cybersecurity is the top barrier to manufacturing AI adoption: Cisco's 2026 industrial AI report, published this week, found that while AI has already improved productivity, quality and resilience in manufacturing, unreliable networks and cybersecurity concerns are the primary barriers to further expansion. The finding reframes the manufacturing AI adoption challenge: the constraint is not model capability but the underlying network and security infrastructure that connects AI to production systems.

For manufacturers evaluating AI deployment, the implication is that investment in network reliability and cybersecurity must precede or accompany AI rollout – not follow it. The report positions Cisco's own networking and security products as the prerequisite infrastructure layer, but the finding is consistent with broader industry evidence. Factories that move AI workloads on-prem – behind their own firewalls – face a compound challenge: they must secure both the AI models and the operational technology networks those models interact with.

[IQVIA to acquire Charles River drug-discovery assets](https://www.pharmaceutical-technology.com/news/iqvia-acquire-charles-river-assets/)

IQVIA signed an agreement to acquire selected discovery service assets from Charles River Laboratories, including five sites specialising in in vitro drug-discovery services, new approach methodologies and a small-molecule AI platform. The assets carry over 20 years of scientific and operational data and have contributed to more than 100 molecules progressing into clinical trials, including several commercially approved drugs.

The acquisition creates an integrated drug-discovery platform spanning lead optimisation, hit-to-lead, target identification and early safety assessment. IQVIA Laboratories president David Morris said the deal 'meaningfully strengthens our ability to support clients earlier in the R&D lifecycle.' The strategic logic is data accumulation: by owning both the physical laboratory infrastructure and the AI platform trained on decades of experimental results, IQVIA is building a vertically integrated discovery offering where each new client engagement generates data that improves the platform's predictive accuracy for subsequent programmes. The transaction is expected to close in Q2 2026.
story_a.heading: IQVIA to acquire Charles River drug-discovery assets

### [xAI launches Grok 4.20 Heavy with 16 specialised agents, backed by $3bn from Saudi's Humain](https://www.eonmsk.com/2026/02/18/xai-launches-grok-4-20-heavy-with-16-specialized-agents/)

xAI released Grok 4.20 Heavy, a multi-agent product that deploys 16 specialised agents to work collaboratively on research projects. The product is gated behind the SuperGrok Heavy subscription tier, positioning it as a premium offering aimed at users willing to pay for orchestrated agent output rather than single-model responses.

Separately, CNBC reported that Saudi Arabia's Humain [invested $3bn in xAI's Series E round](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/19/saudis-humain-invested-3-billion-in-xais-series-e-funding-round.html). The investment continues a pattern of sovereign wealth capital flowing into frontier model companies – Humain joins a funding ecosystem that includes the SpaceX acquisition of xAI announced on 2 February. xAI now has access to SpaceX's infrastructure and sovereign capital, a combination that reduces its dependence on traditional venture financing and public cloud providers.

The 16-agent architecture is notable because it moves beyond single-model deployment toward a system where multiple models negotiate subtasks. If this architecture proves effective, it pressures other providers to ship comparable multi-agent products – increasing the compute cost per query and the infrastructure requirements for competitive parity.
story_a.heading: xAI launches Grok 4.20 Heavy with 16 specialised agents, backed by $3bn from Saudi's Humain

story_b.text: Sectra acquires Oxipit, advancing autonomous AI capabilities in diagnostic imaging News provided by Sectra Mar 05, 2026, 03:03 ET Share this article Share toX Share this article Share toX LINKÖPING, Sweden, March 5, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- International medical imaging IT and cybersecurity company Sectra (STO: SECT B) has entered into an agreement to acquire Oxipit, UAB, a Lithuanian company specializing in AI-based solutions for radiology. Oxipit holds the first CE Class IIB certification for autonomous AI in chest X-ray analysis. The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions and is expected to be completed during March 2026. Most AI in radiology today acts as decision support, flagging findings, suggesting measurements, or assisting prioritization. These tools support radiologists but do not replace any part of the reporting process. Autonomous AI represents a different model. It is developed and validated to independently complete a specific diagnostic task under controlled clinical conditions. The scope of autonomy is clearly defined, performance thresholds are rigorously validated, and use is governed by regulatory approval. Oxipit's ChestLink is a leading example of this autonomous approach. Within its defined clinical scope, it is designed to automatically identify and clear high-confidence normal chest X-ray examinations from the radiologist's worklist. This allows radiologists to focus their expertise on cases with a higher probability of disease. By safely reducing routine workload, this model directly addresses the growing challenge of rising imaging volumes and persistent workforce constraints, while operating under rigorously validated safety thresholds. ChestLink holds CE Class IIb certification*, reflecting the elevated regulatory and clinical validation standards required for autonomous AI applications in healthcare. ChestLink is already available through Sectra Amplifier Marketplace for AI, supporting seamless integration into existing rad
story_b.heading: Sectra acquires Oxipit, advancing autonomous AI capabilities in diagnostic imaging

story_a.text:
[Google publishes real-world diagnostic AI feasibility study](https://research.google/blog/exploring-the-feasibility-of-conversational-diagnostic-ai-in-a-real-world-clinical-study/)

Google Research published results from a real-world clinical study evaluating conversational diagnostic AI – an agent that conducts patient interviews and generates differential diagnoses through dialogue rather than structured data entry. The study, led by Research Lead Mike Schaekermann and Director Alan Karthikesalingam, moves Google's diagnostic work from internal benchmarks to clinical evidence generation.

The publication has regulatory significance. Diagnostic AI tools that can cite real-world feasibility data have a stronger pathway to regulatory clearance than those relying solely on retrospective validation. For health system procurement teams, the study creates a reference point against which competing diagnostic agents will be measured. Google's advantage here is not just the model – it is the clinical study infrastructure and publication record that competitors would need years to replicate.
story_a.heading: Google publishes real-world diagnostic AI feasibility study

story_a.text:
[Decoy Therapeutics and Quantori build Google Cloud-native AI peptide platform](https://www.stocktitan.net/news/DCOY/decoy-therapeutics-enters-strategic-partnership-with-quantori-to-2aiyjhpm5ftd.html)

Decoy Therapeutics (Nasdaq: DCOY) entered a strategic partnership with Quantori to deploy a Google Cloud-native integrated platform combining AI-driven peptide design and molecular simulation. The platform places agentic molecular modelling inside Decoy's discovery workflow rather than running it as a separate analysis step. Each simulation cycle generates training data that feeds back into the model, creating a tightening loop between design iteration and computational validation.

The Google Cloud dependency is a double-edged sword: it gives Decoy access to scalable infrastructure without capital expenditure, but it also means the platform's performance is tied to Google's pricing and service roadmap. For competitors evaluating similar architectures, the choice between cloud-native and self-hosted infrastructure is now a strategic fork with long-term data-sovereignty implications.
story_a.heading: Decoy Therapeutics and Quantori build Google Cloud-native AI peptide platform

### [Doosan Robotics expands AI-driven cobot systems for industrial automation](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/doosan-robotics-cobot-automation)

South Korea's Doosan Robotics expanded its range of AI-driven collaborative robot systems designed for industrial automation. The cobots are intended for deployment alongside human workers on production lines, handling tasks that require both precision and adaptability.

The expansion matters because the cobot market is a proxy for how quickly manufacturing operations can absorb AI. Unlike software agents that can be deployed in hours, cobots require physical installation, safety certification and integration with existing production systems. Each deployment generates operational data that can improve subsequent installations – creating a flywheel effect for companies that accumulate enough deployments. Doosan's challenge is competing with Universal Robots, FANUC and emerging Chinese cobot makers for the early deployments that generate this data advantage.
story_b.heading: Doosan Robotics expands AI-driven cobot systems for industrial automation

[Ryght AI compresses clinical trial site selection from six months to 26 days](https://www.morningstar.com/news/pr-newswire/20260311la07172/ryght-ai-to-host-webinar-revealing-how-a-global-cro-used-ai-to-compress-6-months-of-clinical-trial-site-selection-into-26-days)

Ryght AI disclosed that a global CRO used its platform to compress clinical trial site selection from six months to 26 days – a 7x reduction. The metric is specific enough to change procurement conversations. Site selection is one of the highest-latency steps in trial initiation, and a 7x acceleration directly reduces the cash burn between IND filing and first patient enrolled.

The competitive implication is straightforward: CROs that adopt agent-driven site selection can quote shorter timelines to sponsors, winning mandates from CROs still relying on manual processes. The data generated by each site selection cycle – which sites performed well, which geographies enrolled fastest – becomes a proprietary dataset that improves future selections, rewarding early adopters with a compounding accuracy advantage.
story_a.heading: Ryght AI compresses clinical trial site selection from six months to 26 days

story_a.text:
[AI patents emerge as competitive weapon in insurance](https://www.insurancethoughtleadership.com/ai-machine-learning/ai-patents-emerge-competitive-weapon)

An analysis published this week argued that AI patents represent the next competitive frontier for insurance carriers – and that most are unprepared. An estimated three carriers have filed 77% of all insurance AI patents, predominantly in the P&C space. The concentration is significant: carriers with strong underwriting discipline are using AI to amplify existing advantages, creating a widening gap between patent holders and the rest of the market.

The analysis identified both defensive and offensive dimensions. Defensively, a patented AI tool creates a moat that prevents competitors from replicating a carrier's operational advantage. Offensively, carriers could license patented AI tools to competitors, knowing the licensing carrier's proprietary data gives it a structural edge even when the technology is shared. For the majority of carriers that have not yet filed AI patents, the window to establish competitive positions is narrowing as patent holders accumulate both legal protection and the training data generated by deployed tools.
story_a.heading: AI patents emerge as competitive weapon in insurance

[EXL launches agentic AI for claims acceleration](https://www.stocktitan.net/news/EXLS/exl-unveils-new-agentic-ai-solutions-to-accelerate-enterprise-qekqrkyui0vf.html)

EXL (Nasdaq: EXLS) unveiled a suite of agentic AI solutions on 11 March designed to accelerate claims payouts and shorten end-to-end claims cycle times. The agents sit inside the claims processing workflow, handling tasks from first notice of loss through to settlement calculation. EXL is positioning these agents not as analytical overlays but as operational participants that execute steps in the claims process autonomously.

For carriers, the decision to deploy EXL's agents involves a trade-off: faster cycle times and lower handling costs versus dependence on a third-party agent that accumulates proprietary knowledge about the carrier's claims patterns. Carriers with strong internal AI teams may prefer to build similar capabilities in-house to retain the data advantage. Those without that capacity face the choice of licensing EXL's agents – and accepting that EXL's model improves with every claim processed across its entire client base, not just theirs.
